coredata.dc:description: The quantum confinement and size effects make graphene quantum dots (GQDs) remarkable candidates for sensing applications. Gas molecules can easily be adsorbed on GQDs because of their large specific areas and exposed edges. This computational work concerns the adsorption performance of pristine and transition metal [chromium (Cr) or nickel (Ni)]-doped coronene GQD to hazardous gases carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), hydrogen cyanide (HCN), and cyanogen chloride (CNCl), along with structural properties such as bond length and angle, electronic properties including molecular orbital analysis, natural bond orbital and Mulliken charge analysis, spectroscopic properties of infrared and ultraviolet spectra, quantum theory of atoms in molecules with non-covalent interaction analysis, and sensing properties of recovery time, work function, and sensing response. As pristine GQDs show poor gas adsorption, doping with Cr and Ni enhance performance. The results of Cr-doped coronene GQD indicate that the gas molecules have strong interaction with GQDs as the computed adsorption energies are highly negative (−3.56, −3.81, −4.02, and −4.81 eV). Since the computed recovery times are extremely long, the Cr-doped GQDs can be used as removers of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gas molecules from specific environments. In the case of Ni-doped GQDs, only the interaction with CNCl yields chemisorption, with adsorption energy of −0.83 eV. However, the short recovery time (9.36 ms–93.6 s) indicates its potential candidature as a CNCl sensor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The quantum confinement and size effects make graphene quantum dots (GQDs) remarkable candidates for sensing applications. Gas molecules can easily be adsorbed on GQDs because of their large specific areas and exposed edges. This computational work concerns the adsorption performance of pristine and transition metal [chromium (Cr) or nickel (Ni)]-doped coronene GQD to hazardous gases carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), hydrogen cyanide (HCN), and cyanogen chloride (CNCl), along with structural properties such as bond length and angle, electronic properties including molecular orbital analysis, natural bond orbital and Mulliken charge analysis, spectroscopic properties of infrared and ultraviolet spectra, quantum theory of atoms in molecules with non-covalent interaction analysis, and sensing properties of recovery time, work function, and sensing response. As pristine GQDs show poor gas adsorption, doping with Cr and Ni enhance performance. The results of Cr-doped coronene GQD indicate that the gas molecules have strong interaction with GQDs as the computed adsorption energies are highly negative (−3.56, −3.81, −4.02, and −4.81 eV). Since the computed recovery times are extremely long, the Cr-doped GQDs can be used as removers of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gas molecules from specific environments. In the case of Ni-doped GQDs, only the interaction with CNCl yields chemisorption, with adsorption energy of −0.83 eV. However, the short recovery time (9.36 ms–93.6 s) indicates its potential candidature as a CNCl sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have gained significant attention in adsorption applications due to their unique properties, such as high surface area, tunable pore size, and enhanced adsorption capacities [1]. Various nanomaterials, including palladium-cadmium bimetallic crystals [2] and co-crystals, have been explored for their exceptional performance in sensing applications. Furthermore, a wide array of nanomaterials, including fullerenes, nanotubes [3], and nanocages [4], have been investigated for their proficiency in adsorbing both organic and biomolecules [5]. Large nanoclusters, e.g. Si160 [6], have demonstrated promise in ester adsorption. Emerging materials such as antimonene nanosheets [7] and MnFeO4, characterized by their ferrite surfaces [8], have proven effective for sensing organic molecules, including acetate and acetone. Organic nanomaterials like diaminomaleonitride [9] and 2D materials such as ZnSO4 [10] have found utility in sensing hazardous gases like NO2 and SO2 [11].

Due to the obvious threat of hazardous gases, their detection is very important. Various gases produced from chemical factories are very dangerous to human health and there many hazardous gases in the environment. Among these, the goal is to identify and remove carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), hydrogen cyanide (HCN), and cyanogen chloride (CNCl) gases from specific surroundings. In sub-quality natural gas, CO2 and H2S are two hazardous contaminants [12]. Although CO2 has many applications in the food and beverage industry, global warming and ocean acidification are some of the CO2 gas emission disadvantages to the environment [13]. In a gaseous state, H2S is extremely hazardous to health. Since it is heavier than air, it can collect in low areas, such as the bottom of storage or cargo vessels [14]. Cyanides are multi-faceted poisons, including a fire smoke toxin, and are used in suicide, murder, and terrorism, as well as posing occupational and industrial risks [15]. Both HCN and CNCl are toxic compounds with low boiling points and high volatility, most likely to be found in gaseous form at room temperature [16,17]. Therefore, monitoring these gases is essential to prevent environmental damage.

Researchers have used many carbon-based nanomaterials for gas-sensing applications in the past. For example, Yoon et al. used graphene sheet for CO2 sensing [18], Zhang et al. explored boron-doped carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as cyanide sensors [19], and Li et al. explored the H2S-sensing properties of pristine and doped graphene sheet [20]. Recently, Vadalkar et al. explored the HCN-sensing properties of pristine and Al-, Si-, and P-decorated C18 nanoclusters [21]. Novel materials are always sought for sensing applications since a good gas sensor requires optimal adsorption properties with appropriate adsorption energy and adsorption distance. The quantum confinement and size effects make graphene quantum dots (GQDs) remarkable candidates for sensing applications [22]. Gas molecules can easily be adsorbed on GQDs because of their large specific areas and exposed edges [23]. In the recent past, numerous chemical and biomolecule sensing methods have been investigated using quantum dots including for coronene [24,25], corranulene [26], and ovalene [27].

Carbon allotropes, including C60 fullerenes decorated with gallium and indium atoms [28] as well as metal-modified structures like nickel (Ni)-doped C24 fullerenes encapsulated with alkaline metals [29], have exhibited remarkable versatility in addressing environmental challenges. These materials have shown promise as effective absorbers of organic pollutants and have demonstrated excellent sensing capabilities, notably in thiourea detection [30]. Moreover, a comprehensive literature survey showed a broader and recurrent trend within the realm of carbon allotropes, encompassing a diverse array of structures including CNTs [31], graphene nanoribbons [32], GQDs [33], graphyne sheets [34], carbon nanoclusters [35], and other carbon-based materials. This recurring pattern underscores the notable enhancement in their adsorption performance, an improvement that remains remarkably consistent and reproducible when impurity doping and decoration strategies are thoughtfully applied.

Al-Otaibi et al. investigated adsorption of 2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one on the coronene GQD surface using density functional theory (DFT) simulations [36]. Malloum et al. assessed the effects of solvation and temperature on the adsorption of phenol onto coronene [37]. Agwamba et al. investigated the structural and electronic sensitivity of engineered nanosensors to adsorbing arsenide gas and the effect of encapsulating a few selected cations (K, Al, Mg, and Ca) on the coronene GQD surfaces [38]. Apart from these studies, a theoretical study by Malcek et al. indicates the stability of transition metal (TM)-doped GQDs [39]. The best suited dopants for carbon-based nanomaterials, such as C20 fullerene, are thought to be chromium (Cr) and Ni. The enhanced interaction capabilities of C20 fullerene with the doping of Cr and Ni were examined in-depth by Rad et al. [40] in their extended research on a C20 fullerene-based DNA biosensor. Liao et al. [41] demonstrated how addition of germanium and Cr improved the adsorption capabilities of graphene. According to Lu et al. [42], CNTs have demonstrated improved sensing capabilities after Ni doping. Previous research has shown that TM doping can enhance adsorption of various molecules over the carbon allotropes. However, adsorption behavior of hazardous gases like CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl over TM-doped coronene GQDs remains unexplored, and this work explores the adsorption performance of Cr- and Ni-doped coronene GQDs along with the pristine structure to the aforementioned hazardous gases using first-principles-based DFT.

The 6-31G (d,p) level theory and the gradient-corrected functional B3LYP, which is consistent with the reported data [43], are used to optimize the structure of GQD and gas molecules. The Gaussian 09 package [79] is used for all DFT calculations, including those involving total energies, electronic parameters, electrostatic potential, and molecular orbitals (MOs). GaussView version 6 is employed for the visualization of structures and Mulliken charges [44]. The GaussSum tool [45] is used to plot density of states and infrared (IR) spectra. The Multiwfn [46] package is employed to plot the reduced density gradient and visual molecular dynamics (VMD) software is employed for visualization of non-covalent interactions (NCIs) [47]. During the calculation, the convergence criteria are 12 × 10−4 radian, 18 × 1 0−4 Bohr, 3 × 10−4 Hartree/radian, and 45 × 10−5 Hartree/Bohr for gradients of root-mean-square (rms) displacement, for maximum displacement, for rms force, and for maximum force, respectively.

The difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is called the HOMO-LUMO gap (EG), given as follows [48]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and electronic properties of pristine and TM-doped coronene GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Before studying the adsorption performance of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gases on coronene GQDs, the structural stability of pristine and Cr- and Ni-doped coronene is clarified with the help of IR plot and calculating formation energy [49]:
(3)
E
f o r m
=
E
c o r o n e n e
− x
E C
− y
E H
− z
E
d o p a n t
x + y + z
,
where
E
f o r m
is formation energy of the system;
E
c o r o n e n e
is total energy of coronene GQD;

,
E H
,
and E
d o p a n t
represent the corresponding energy of individual C, H, and impurity atoms (i.e. Cr and Ni), respectively; and

represent the total number of C, H, and impurity atoms, respectively. The formation energies of pristine and Cr- and Ni-substituted coronene GQDs are −7.01 eV (C24H12), −6.74 eV (C23H12Cr), and −6.81 eV (C23H12Ni), respectively. Negative values of formation energy ensure stability of the structure. Doping with Cr and Ni in pristine coronene marginally decreases the structural stability but does not affect adsorption properties of coronene GQDs. Fig. 1 presents the optimized structures of coronene and Cr- and Ni-doped coronene GQDs. The polarity of a bond is determined by the dipole moment (DM). All non-polar molecules have zero DM, and this is used to distinguish between polar and non-polar bonds. It is also helpful in calculating the percentage ionic character of a molecule [50]. The DM of pristine coronene is zero, indicating a symmetrical charge distribution inside pristine coronene. In contrast, the doping of Cr and Ni atoms in pristine coronene will raise the DM. Table 1 shows the formation energy, DM, point group, HOMO, LUMO, and Eg of pristine and TM-doped coronene. The point group for all structures is found to exhibit the C1 point group, which has only identity operation. Moreover, Eg of pristine coronene is 4.02 eV, which is comparable with previously reported work [51]. Upon substitution, the dopant will decrease Eg, which arises due to the overlapping of C atoms with dopant atoms (Cr or Ni).

The functional groups of molecules can be determined by analyzing atom vibrations using IR spectroscopy. The areas where molecules absorb IR radiation are estimated. Moreover, peaks in an IR plot indicate regions of the spectrum where specific bond vibrations occur, and the absence of an imaginary frequency suggests that a system of molecules is dynamically stable [52]. Fig. S2 (from the supporting information) presents the IR spectra of pristine and Cr- and Ni-doped coronene. All frequencies in three plots are positive, which proves structural stability. It is evident from the observation that adding a dopant to pristine coronene can change its structural stability and electrical characteristics. The Cartesian diagram and coordinates of the base systems are provided in the Supporting Information Section 9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl on pristine and TM-doped coronene GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Here, the interaction of adsorbed gas molecules with pristine C24H12 and doped C23H12X (X = Cr, Ni) is examined. The CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gas molecules are adsorbed at various sites of pristine C24H12 and doped C23H12X (X = Cr, Ni). After several orientations (based on the active sites, see Supporting Information Section 1) of each gas molecule over the system, the most stable orientation (i.e. with lowest energy after optimization) is illustrated in Figs. 2–4
and their adsorption parameters are listed in Table 2 . The interaction between gas molecules and pristine/doped coronene can be visualized by their adsorption energies. The adsorption energy is calculated using the following equation [53]:
(4)
E
a d
=
E
s y s t e m
+
g a s

E
s y s t e m
−
E
g a s

Here, the systems are pristine (C24H12), Cr-doped (C23H12Cr), and Ni-doped (C23H12Ni) coronene GQDs. Adsorbed gas molecules are CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl. The Ead, Esystem + gas, Esystem, and Egas are adsorption energy, total energy of the gas-adsorbed system, total energy of the system, and total energy of isolated gas molecule, respectively. Thermodynamic studies of the adsorption process are discussed in detail in the Supporting Information Section 6. Table 2 shows the adsorption energies, HOMO, LUMO, Eg, Fermi level, and DM of pristine and doped GQDs before and after adsorption. For Cr-doped GQD, the order of adsorption energy follows: −4.81 eV (CNCl) > −4.02 eV (HCN) ≈ −3.81 eV (CO2) −0.10 (H2S) > −0.08 (CO2). The adsorption energy is the least negative for the Ni-doped coronene system and the most negative for the Cr-doped system. Thus, the adsorption capacity of the Ni-doped system toward these hazardous gases is moderate compared to Cr-doped GQDs. All systems have negative adsorption energies, implying that all processes are exothermic and the systems are capable of adsorption of gas molecules. More negative adsorption energies suggest more strongly adsorbed gas molecules. Change of adsorption energy is negligible in the case of pristine coronene. Hence we conclude that pristine coronene interacts in the same way with all these types of gas molecules. More variation of adsorption energies is shown by Cr-doped coronene, for which the CNCl molecule shows the most negative adsorption energy (−4.81 eV). Correction to the basis set superposition error and empirical dispersion is discussed in the Supporting Information Sections 3 and 4.

Large variation in initial and final separation occurs when gas molecules are adsorbed over the system. Here, defined adsorption distance is the final separation after the adsorption. Supporting Information Section 2 shows the adsorption distances in a tabulated form. The change in adsorption distances is from 2.34 to 3.81, 1.80 to 5.31, 2.84 to 2.66, and 2.00 to 2.83 Å when CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gas molecules are adsorbed in pristine coronene, respectively. Also, the corresponding changes when adsorbed over Cr-doped coronene are from 2.34 to 2.03, 2.71 to 2.36, 1.73 to 1.97, and 2.00 to 1.95 Å; and when adsorbed in Ni-doped coronene are 2.81–3.22, 4.01 to 2.84, 1.33 to 2.80, and 2.00 to 1.88 Å. This change in adsorption distance is due to the interaction of gas molecules with the system. The adsorption distances are very large, and adsorption energies are negligible; highlighting that the interaction of the pristine GQD with the gas molecules is weak, hence it is not a suitable candidate as a hazardous gas sensor. Figs. 5 and 6
show the bond length and angle alterations for the pristine and doped GQDs before and after adsorption, with a detailed description provided in Supporting Information Section 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic properties of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl adsorbed in TM-doped coronene GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Mulliken charge transfer takes place due to the molecular adsorption of gases over the GQDs [54]. For C23H12Cr interaction with the gas molecules, the charge is transferred from the gas molecule to the GQD only in the case of H2S adsorption (+0.072 e); whereas in the other three cases, charge transfer is the other way around. The maximum charge transfer is after the CO adsorption over Cr-doped GQDs (−0.338 e). In the case of Ni-doped GQDs, the charge is transferred from the gas molecule to the GQD only in the case of CO2 adsorption (+0.007 e); whereas in the other three cases, charge transfer is the other way around. The maximum charge transfer, in this case, is after the CNCl adsorption (−0.028 e). The variations in Mulliken charges before and after adsorption are tabulated as well (see Table 3 ).

Furthermore, the electric DM vector gives insight into the charge distribution. The DM is not shown by pristine coronene due to the equal distribution of the charge. The substitutional doping of Cr and Ni in pristine coronene slightly disturbs the charge symmetry (i.e. negligible change in DM). There is a drastic change in DM after the adsorption of gas molecules on pristine and TM-doped coronene. The adsorption of CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl gas molecules over pristine coronene changes DM from 0 to 0.19, 1.68, 3.69, and 3.87 Debye, respectively. However, for Cr-doped coronene, the corresponding DM changes are 0.05–3.91, 4.12, 5.43, and 2.84 Debye; and for Ni-doped coronene DM are 0.06–1.04, 2.75, 4.26, and 4.56 Debye. The change in the DM also supports that the charge transfer occurs after gas adsorption for each case.

To obtain a deep insight into the interaction between gas molecules and TM-doped coronene, EHOMO, ELUMO, EF, ΔEF, EG, and ΔEG are calculated (Table 2). The computed EG is 4.02 eV for pristine GQD, which agrees with the reported data [55]. Analysis of MO populations (density of states) for gas molecules adsorbed to TM-doped GQDs is also conducted. There is negligible change in EG via the adsorption of gas molecules over pristine coronene; whereas for CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl adsorption over Cr-doped coronene, EG increases by 26.42 %, 29.53 %, 24.35 %, and 31.61 %, respectively; and corresponding values over Ni-doped coronene decrease by 6.69 %, 5.51 %, 6.30 %, and 13.78 %. This is consistent with the conclusion drawn from the MO spectra (Fig. 7 ), which combined with Table 2 shows a change in Fermi energy and consequent shifting in the corresponding Fermi level. In Table 2, positive and negative signs indicate increment and decrement over the original value, respectively. Fig. 7 reveals that the contribution of HOMO increases, indicating a chemisorption nature [56]. If the change in MO population is negligible, this indicates a physisorption nature. Those gas molecules adsorbed over Cr-doped coronene show a chemisorption nature and all others exhibit a physisorption nature. The major contribution to the HOMO and LUMO side near the Fermi level is from the TM-doped coronene GQDs (Fig. 7), which indicates minimal hybridization for the gas molecule in the gas–GQD complex, pointing to a van der Waals type interaction. In the case of CNCl adsorption over Cr-doped GQDs, the major contribution is from the gas molecule, suggesting strong hybridization and hence a covalent-type interaction [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Natural bond orbital (NBO) and natural hybrid orbital (NHO) analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Weak occupancies of valence anti-bonds indicate non-negligible deviations from an idealized localized Lewis structure, signifying true delocalization phenomena. The NBO analysis offers an exceptional means to construct a highly accurate depiction of the Lewis structure, as it systematically selects orbitals that encompass the maximum electron density. A salient feature of the NBO method is its capacity to furnish insights into interactions occurring within both occupied and virtual orbital spaces, thus enriching the comprehension of intra- and inter-molecular interactions [58]. In this study, NBO calculations [59] are executed employing the NBO 3.1 program incorporated into the Gaussian 09 suite, utilizing the DFT/B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) method. This methodology provides a solid foundation for probing charge transfer and conjugative interactions within molecular systems and offers an effective approach to evaluate bond interactions. Notably, a higher stabilization energy value implies a more pronounced interaction between electron donors and acceptors, reflecting an increased propensity for electron donation from donors to acceptors and an extended conjugation across the entire molecular system. The NBO analysis has already demonstrated its efficacy as a valuable tool for interpreting hyperconjugative interactions and the transfer of electron density from filled lone-pair electrons. Hyperconjugative interaction energies are deduced using a second-order perturbation approach:
(5)
E 2
=
q i
F
i j
2
E i
−
E j
where qi is the donor orbital occupancy, ej and ei are diagonal elements, and F(i, j) is the off-diagonal NBO Fock matrix element.

The second-order perturbation energy (E2) exhibits its highest value in the context of gas sensing for the Cr-doped coronene system (see Table 4 ). This observation underscores a significant interaction and substantial charge transfer between gas molecules and C23H12Cr, a phenomenon corroborated by Millikan charge analysis. The trend in E2 values follows the order of H2S (133.89 kcal/mol) > HCN (110.19 kcal/mol) > CO2 (86.74 kcal/mol) > CNCl (67.84 kcal/mol). This ranking reflects the degree of interaction between these gases and the C23H12Cr system. Furthermore, the pronounced adsorption energy values signify a chemisorption nature between these entities. For instance, CNCl exhibits a robust adsorption with a second-order stabilization energy of 58.17 kcal/mol when interacting with C23H12Ni, reaffirming the substantial interaction between CNCl and Ni-doped coronene. In contrast, all other values are negligible, indicative of moderate interaction and a physisorption nature in those cases.

The NHOs arise from the symmetric orthogonalization of hybrid orbitals, which are obtained through a unitary transformation of natural atomic orbitals centered on specific atoms. In accordance with the basic bond orbital framework, a NBO is defined as an orbital originating from NHOs. Consequently, for a localized σ-bond linking atoms A and B, the NBO can be expressed as follows:
(6)
σ
A B
=
c A
h A
+
c B
h B
where, hA and hB represent the natural hybrids centered on atoms A and B, respectively, while cA and cB denote the polarization coefficients associated with atoms A and B. The orientation of each hybrid orbital is specified using spherical polar angles, θ and φ, originating from the nucleus, in addition to the deviation angle (Dev) relative to the line connecting the nuclei centers. For more complex
s
p λ
d μ
hybrids, the hybrid orbital orientation is determined numerically to maximize angular amplitude and subsequently compared with the direction of the line connecting the two nuclei centers to ascertain bond bending. This bending is quantified as Dev (°) between these two directions. The angular characteristics of natural hybrid orbitals are strongly influenced by the type of substituents, which can induce conjugative or steric effects. In the tables of Supporting Information Section 8, the bending angles of various bonds are expressed as the degree of deviation from the direction connecting the centers of the nuclei.

Time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) is a computational quantum chemistry method used to compute electronic excited state properties for molecules and materials. It is commonly applied to predict and analyze UV spectra, which reveal electronic transitions within molecules. Peaks in UV spectra correspond to transitions between MOs, notably from HOMO to LUMO, yielding prominent peaks whose positions provide information about the ground-to-excited state energy gap. The intensity of these peaks is associated with the oscillator strength, a measure of transition probability; stronger transitions exhibit higher oscillator strengths and result in more intense peaks. Moreover, TD-DFT UV spectra analysis can elucidate the nature of electronic transitions (e.g. π → π* or n → π* transitions) and offer insights into the molecule's overall electronic structure.

To analyze the UV spectra, TD-DFT calculations are performed using 6–31 G (d, p) B3LYP level theory [48,60,61]. Figs. S4 and S5 display the UV spectra for a gas adsorbate system, specifically focusing on the adsorption peaks of gas molecule adsorbates over TM-doped coronene. These adsorption peaks provide critical insights into the interaction between gas molecules and the carbon-based substrate. Among the observed peaks (Table 5 ), one at 881.47 nm is particularly intense with an oscillator strength of 0.0006 when CO2 gas is adsorbed over Ni-doped coronene. This peak represents a specific wavelength at which the interaction between CO2 and the substrate is highly pronounced. The discrepancy in the peak position suggests a shift in the electronic structure of the adsorbate–substrate system. The phenomenon of shifting absorption peaks toward shorter wavelengths is termed “blue shift,” indicating a higher energy interaction between the gas molecules and the substrate. In contrast, when a peak shifts toward longer wavelengths, this is referred to as a “red shift,” signifying a lower energy interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) study
ce:section.ce:para: To gain insight into the interactions between gas molecules and TM-doped coronene, a topological analysis based on Bader's quantum theory of atoms in molecules method is employed, utilizing the Multiwfn package [46]. Assessment of inter-molecular interactions within this system relies on topology parameters, which are computed based on electron density at critical bond points through QTAIM analysis (Table 6 ). Within QTAIM analysis, the electron density ρ(r) and Laplacian ∇2ρ(r) are frequently employed characteristics for deciphering the nature of bonding interactions. However, particularly noteworthy parameters pertaining to bonding characteristics include the total energy density H(r) and the ratio of |V(r)|/[G(r)]. It is noteworthy that these parameters reveal distinct characteristics for various types of interactions. Weak and medium-strength hydrogen bonding as well as van der Waals interactions are identified when ∇2ρ(r) > 0, H(r) > 0, and |V(r)|/[G(r)] < 1. In the case of strong hydrogen bonds, an intermediate type of interaction, the characteristics are ∇2ρ(r) > 0, H(r) < 0, and 1 < |V(r)|/[G(r)] < 2. Lastly, covalent bonding characteristics manifest when ∇2ρ(r) < 0, H(r) < 0, and |V(r)|/[G(r)] > 2 [62,63].

The calculated QTAIM parameters for the TM-doped coronene are given in Table 6. The computed molecular topographical maps of the gas molecules over TM-doped coronene with critical points are illustrated in Figs. S6 and S7. The computation results (Table 6) show four typical interactions between the gas molecule and the system. The of H2S–C23H12Cr, CO2–C23H12Cr, CNCl–C23H12Cr, and CNCl–C23H12Ni interactions can be classified as intermediate interactions of semi-covalent character due to the ∇2ρ(r) > 0, H(r) < 0, 1 < |V(r)|/[G(r)] < 2 rule. The other QTAIM parameters correspond to weak hydrogen bonding interactions because the topology parameters reveal only the electrostatic nature of those bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced density gradient (RDG) plots and NCI analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The RDG plots are a valuable analytical tool for comprehending the attractive and repulsive aspects of molecular interactions, and to assess and characterize the strength of such interactions [60]. The sign (λ2) values, when positive, signify strong attractive forces within the charge density, while negative values indicate repulsive forces. When ρ = 0, this corresponds to van der Waals forces. In the NCI plot, distinct regions colored blue, green, and red denote strong, van der Waals, and intense steric interactions, respectively (Figs. 8 and 9
). The interaction between the gas molecules and GQDs is evident in the context of van der Waals interactions [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing properties of TM-doped coronene GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing (or sensitivity) properties of TM-doped coronene toward the selected gas molecules can be obtained by analyzing the conductivity, given by the following equation [48]:
(7)
σ = A
T
3 2
exp
(
−
E G
2
K T
)
,
where σ, A, EG, K, and T are the electrical conductivity, constant (electrons/m3K3/2), HOMO-LUMO gap, Boltzmann constant, and temperature, respectively. From the above equation, at a given temperature, electrical conductivity is inversely proportional to EG meaning that electric conductivity increases with a decrease in EG (see Table 2). Electrical conductivity is an almost constant value in the case of pristine coronene but decreases for Cr-doped and increases in Ni-doped coronene due to increase and decrease of EG, respectively. The increase in EG increases resistance of the system. The changes in the resistance of the material are also proportional to the concentration of gas molecules present in the environment. The effective interaction of gas molecules significantly changes the electronic properties of the system, indicating its potential use as an electronic sensor for gas molecule detection.

Relative change in conductivity gives the sensing response for the given system. This is purely dependent on a change in EG at constant temperature (here room temperature, T = 300 K) the sensing response is calculated by the following formula [65]:
(8)
S = exp
(
| Δ
E G
|
2
K T
)
− 1
,
where ΔEG, K, and T are changes in EG, the Boltzmann constant, and temperature, respectively. The Cr-doped coronene gives maximum sensing response, for all four gases. This implies that C23H12Cr is the most suitable system for sensing CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl molecules. Also, the sensing response in Ni-doped coronene cannot be eliminated. So, C23H12Ni is also useful to detect these hazardous gas molecules.

Another important characteristic of the sensors of gas molecules is recovery time. Recovery time of any gas sensor may be defined as the expected time until the desorption takes place from its host system. Shorter recovery times are expected for a good gas sensor. A strong interaction between the gas molecules and the sensor is not good for a gas sensor because the desorption process of the gas molecules will be difficult and so the sensor needs longer to recover. The recovery time is obtained by the following formula [48,66]:
(9)
τ =
ν 0

,
where τ,

for temperature, T = 300 K. Calculated recovery times (using above equation) for pristine coronene are in the millisecond range and for Ni-doped coronene are in the nano/pico/femto second range, and there is very large recovery time, attempt frequency, adsorption energy, Boltzmann constant, and temperature. For the recovery times, three different attempt frequencies (
ν 0
)
are used (1012, 5.2 × 1014, and 1016 Hz for IR, yellow, and UV light, respectively) of Cr- and Ni-doped coronene (Table 7 ). The calculated recovery times are very large, showing that C23H12Cr has permanently adsorbed all gas molecules as mentioned above. Hence the Cr-doped coronene system can be employed as a gas remover from a specific environment. The short recovery time and chemisorption nature of Ni-doped coronene suggests that it is a good candidate for sensor application [67].

The gas adsorptions influence the Fermi level and work function (ɸ) of the materials; inspection of crucial factors for a ɸ-type sensor is essential. The ɸ-type sensors apply a Kelvin oscillator instrument to estimate the ɸ-values after and before a gas molecule adsorption process. If the gas adsorption raises or lower the ɸ of a sensor, it interrupts the gate voltage, creating an electrical signal. The ɸ is defined as the minimum amount of energy required to remove an electron from a material to a point in the vacuum immediately outside the solid surface and is given by the following equation:
(10)
φ =
V ∞
−
E F
,
where
V ∞
and EF are electrostatic potential energy far from the material surface and Fermi energy, respectively. From the above equation, if
V ∞
= 0, this implies ɸ = −EF. The variation in the value of ɸ of an adsorbent system during the gas adsorption process changes its field emission characteristics, which can be correlated using a classical Richardson–Dushman equation. The current density of emitted electrons in vacuum is given by the Richardson–Dushman equation [68]:
(11)
j = A
T 2
exp
(
−
φ
K T
)
,
where A is the Richardson constant (A/m2) and T is temperature. After gas adsorption, due to the interaction between gas molecules and system, there is a change in the HOMO and LUMO energies of Cr- and Ni-doped coronene. Due to changes in HOMO and LUMO energies, the ɸ and EF also change (Table 2). The value of ɸ decreases by 13.36 % when CO2 and CNCl gas adsorb over C23H12Cr but increases by 15.25 % and 17.41 % when H2S and HCN gas adsorb, respectively. In the case of Ni-doped coronene, ɸ decreases by 11.31 %, 15.74 %, 19.67 %, and 37.21 % for CO2, H2S, HCN, and CNCl, respectively. These results suggest that the C23H12Cr and C23H12Ni can be used as ɸ sensors for these gas molecules because of the significant changes in ɸ (which are not negligible) after adsorption of gas molecules (See Table 7).

The activity of the clusters or molecular systems is investigated by calculating global indices parameters: chemical potential (μ), softness (S), hardness (Ƞ), and electrophilicity (ω). These parameters are given by the following formulas [69]:
(12)

,
where I = −EHOMO and A = −ELUMO. Tabulated global indices parameters are listed in Table 8 . After Cr and Ni doping in pristine coronene, the chemical potens drastically due to changes in the HOMO and LUMO energies of the systems. The chemical potential is highest in the case of CNCl and CO2 adsorption and lowest for HCN adsorption for C23H12Cr. Whereas in the case of C23H12Ni, the highest is CNCl and the lowest is CO2 adsorption. The Ƞ is a measure of the chemical stability of the systems or molecules and the value is 0.97 and 1.27 eV for Cr- and Ni-doped coronene, respectively. The Ƞ increases after the adsorption of gas molecules. This increment in Ƞ is due to an increment in EG after gas adsorption. The S is inversely proportional to Ƞ. The S decreases due to an increment in Ƞ of the gas-adsorbed system. The tendency to accept electrons from atoms or molecules is measured by ω, which has values for C23H12Cr and C23H12Ni of 7.11 and 3.66 eV, respectively. There is fluctuation in ω after adsorption of gas molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of adsorption properties of gas molecules with other previously reported materials
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, a comparative analysis of the sensing capabilities for gas molecules H2S, CO2, HCN, and CNCl is conducted using various effective sensors. This investigation is undertaken in relation to prior research studies, and the summarized findings are presented in Table 9 . It is noteworthy that all assessments are performed at standard room temperature conditions (T = 298.15 K). Among the various sensor materials investigated, C23H12Cr exhibits a notably high adsorption energy for the target gas molecules and displays a substantial alteration in Eg. This characteristic indicates that C23H12Cr is a promising candidate for the study of sensing properties concerning hazardous gas molecules. However, the adsorption energy for C23H12Ni is relatively small, typically less than 1 eV. This suggests a physisorption nature of the interaction between C23H12Ni and the gas molecules under investigation. However, it is important to note that despite the physisorption nature, C23H12Ni displays a significant change in Eg, indicating its potential utility for sensing purposes. In contrast, the pristine material examined in this study exhibits no appropriate adsorption energy for the studied gas molecules. Additionally, the absence of a significant change in Eg in the pristine material implies that it is not well-suited for sensing applications.

Barring CNCl adsorption on Cr-doped coronene GQD, all the adsorption performances do not form chemical bonds, despite yielding appreciable adsorption energies. Those cases can be justified by a drastic change in Eg, implying a greater sensing response. In some cases (for pristine GQD) the adsorption distance exceeds 3 Å, which is not favorable for sensing applications [78]. Owing to the poor adsorption properties of pristine GQD, it is excluded from further calculations such as electronic and sensing properties.

For Cr-doped GQDs, in accordance with the observed data, the highest adsorption energy, shorter adsorption distance, and a significant variation in Eg collectively indicate a chemisorption nature and an order of adsorption energies of CNCl > HCN > CO2 > H2S. Furthermore, the QTAIM parameters also support the conclusion that the interaction between Cr-doped GQDs and gas molecules has a covalent nature. Additionally, in the NBO analysis, the presence of a substantial amount of second-order perturbation energy indicates stable covalent bonding. Both NCI and RDG plots further corroborate these results.

For Ni-doped GQDs, a small value of adsorption energy, variation in Eg, and an appropriate adsorption distance (less than 3 Å) collectively indicate a physisorption nature and the trends of adsorption energy is CNCl > HCN > H2S > CO2. Furthermore, the QTAIM parameters also support the conclusion that the interaction between Ni-doped GQDs and gas molecules exhibits a van der Waals interaction. Additionally, in the NBO analysis, the low amount of second-order perturbation energy suggests weak bonding. The NCI and RDG plots further validate these results.
More negative adsorption energy in the case of C23H12Cr implies longer recovery time, meaning it can be used for gas molecule removal applications.

Section 1 consists of the analysis of molecular electrostatic potential maps. Section 2 contains the tabular data of adsorption distances. The bond length alteration and angle alteration are discussed in detail in Section 3. Section 4 consists of the BSSE corrections. In Section 5, dispersion correction is calculated. Thermodynamic properties are discussed in Section 6. Section 7 contains the calculated IR spectra. Section 8 include the UV spectra. Computed molecular topographical maps are presented in Section 9. The NHOs are tabulated in Section 10. Section 11 contains the Cartesian diagrams and coordinates of base materials.
